  <h2 id="lecture_heading" class="section-title dsp-flex-xs flex-align-items-center-xs" data-lecture-id="32221423" data-next-lecture-id="32221439" data-lecture-url="/courses/465756/lectures/32221423" data-next-lecture-url="/courses/465756/lectures/32221439" ,="" data-previous-lecture-url="/courses/465756/lectures/23737529" data-previous-lecture-id="23737529">

    1. How To Make It Rain (Part I)
</h2>

  <!-- Attachment Blocks -->
<!-- DEBUG: attachments.count=4 -->

  <!-- DEBUG: attachment.id=57338391, kind=text -->
  <div class="lecture-attachment lecture-attachment-type-text" id="lecture-attachment-57338391">
    <div class="attachment-data"></div>
    
      <div class="attachment-data"></div>
<div class="lecture-text-container">
<p></p><p>"The Creative Economy" has exploded. The number of brands and companies that hire creative individuals to help bring their products and their messages out into the world surpasses any other time in history. </p><p>But a few years ago I came across this video that perfectly put into words why so many creatives will continue to get stuck in a downward spiral as soon as they start working with brands.</p><p></p>
</div>

    
  </div>

  <!-- DEBUG: attachment.id=57338430, kind=code_embed -->
  <div class="lecture-attachment lecture-attachment-type-code_embed" id="lecture-attachment-57338430">
    <div class="attachment-data"></div>
    
      <iframe width="560" height="315" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/_2UOBFqOq9Q" title="YouTube video player" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture" allowfullscreen=""></iframe>
    
  </div>

  <!-- DEBUG: attachment.id=57337780, kind=text -->
  <div class="lecture-attachment lecture-attachment-type-text" id="lecture-attachment-57337780">
    <div class="attachment-data"></div>
    
      <div class="attachment-data"></div>
<div class="lecture-text-container">
<p></p><p><span style="color: var(--squid-ink);">In case you find it too long, it's too wordy and and don't like "the messenger", I'm gonna dumb-down the concepts within it in this module. </span></p><p>These are essential BUSINESS ART vitamins if you wanna thrive working with "clients".</p><p>This is "truthlotion" if there was ever was "truthlotion".</p><p style="display: none;"><br class="softbreak"></p><h1><strong>THE BRAND / THE CLIENT</strong></h1><p>So picture, the creative economy, as a classic, three layer cheese cake. </p><p>At the top of the cake is the sour cream topping. </p><p>The brand.</p><p>They're the ones with all the money. </p><p>And all they want in life is to have more people LOVE them and buy their products. And they're ready to spend to make that happen. 'Cause if they succeed. They'll make way more money. </p><p><br class="softbreak"></p><h1><strong>THE TECHNICIAN</strong></h1><p>And at the bottom of the cake is the graham crust. </p><p>The worker-bees. Or The technicians. </p><p>The technicians are the people that do all the daily things required to make the business move forward. They do the daily tasks that allow the business to operate.</p><p>Super important. But let's be honest... pretty much interchangeable.</p><p>As a technician you're only as valuable as the particular task that you do is valuable to the company. There are predetermined budgets for whatever it is that you bring to the table.</p><p>So... Understand... if you make videos for a brand, or take pictures or you DJ for them, or make logos, or really cool illustrations for their packaging or whatever... There will be a set price that people are willing to pay for that thing that you do. And everyone in the industry knows pretty much what that price is. Or the RANGE you can make. And it's basically a take it or leave it kind of thing. cause if it's not you doing... an hour later, they can find someone else Maybe even hand it off to the intern.</p><p>So when things go bad in business, the technicians are the first ones who get their budget slashed... or stop getting hired altogether.</p><p>So what does all this mean for you?</p><p>It means that if you're a technician... you're a commodity.</p><p>If the work you do can be by anyone else, you're a commodity.</p><p>A video guy... is a commodity.</p><p>The house photographer... is a commodity.</p><p>The DJ... a commodity.</p><p>Graphic designer... a commodity.</p><p>The band playing at the holiday party... a commodity</p><p>You may call yourself an artist or whatever.</p><p>But most artists are technicians who can be easily replaced by someone cheaper, someone younger, or someone with nicer gear.</p><p>It's an assembly line.</p><p>The educational systems are cranking out technicians year after year after year.</p><p>And self-taught youtube kids will do what you do faster and work for free to move ahead.</p><p>So while you may call yourself a creative... In the eyes of a brand or a company or a small business or a marketing agency... you are merely a technician.</p><p>And what you do, no matter how pretty it is, no matter how cool it is, no matter how artsy it is... it has a budget range that's been pre-determined by your industry. And you won't be exceeding that number any time soon.</p><p>You... Are in the rat race, just like everyone else that's working a 9 to 5 job. Except you just have a more glamorous title. of course. You get to call yourself a 'creative'.</p><p>Yippee.</p><p>So the million dollar question is?</p><p>Is there a way to escape from this drab role of technician?</p><p>Is there a way to make money beyond the pre-determined suffocating ranges? And STILL be working as a creative?</p><p>Yes, absolutely.</p><p><br class="softbreak"></p><h1><strong>THE RAINMAKER</strong></h1><p>Remember that cheesecake I was talking about?</p><p>At the top is the cream, the brand. At the bottom is the crust, the technicians.</p><p>Well... you need to get yourself in that coveted middle spot. the creamy cheesecake center.</p><p>Say goodbye to graham cracker life.</p><p>It's time to start behaving like</p><p>A rainmaker.</p><p>Yes, that's right.</p><p>You need to become a creative who can "make it rain".</p><p>You gotta re-program your mind so that you can play the real game that's being played.</p><p>From now on, when you start a new working relationship with a brand, your only goal should be to create art that can make it rain.</p><p>Just like Richard Avedon did.</p><p>Just like Helmut Newton did.</p><p>Just like Anton Corbijn.</p><p>And Andy Warhol.</p><p>When you belong to this elite of creatives, you will have no more caps on your earning potential like technicians do. </p><p>You will no longer be a commodity. </p><p>Why?</p><p>Because as a rainmaker, you'll be bringing in more fans to a business, or more customers , or media attention.</p><p>And you know what all of those things mean to the brand?</p><p>MORE MONEY.</p><p>And no matter what they say... that's all they care about. They don't care about your art, unless it's helping them make money.</p><p>So If you want to be the artist who get's paid beyond scale, you'll have to shift your focus from making art, to making art that makes it rain.</p><p>The middle spot of the cheese cake analogy is the sweet spot where you're gonna want to take your career this year.</p><p>That way, in a few months you'll have a manager who will be able to say:</p><p>"If you want what we got, this is the price."</p><p><br class="softbreak"></p><h1><strong>HOW DO YOU DO IT?</strong></h1><p>So how do you do it?</p><p>How do go from being a technician to becoming a rainmaker?</p><p>Especially if you're not mega famous, or an influencer or anything obvious like that?</p><p>How do you make this shift in your career?</p><p>It's all a matter of positioning.</p><p>And There Are 5 ways to do it...</p><p><a href="https://learn.lastnightsparty.tv/courses/enroll/lectures/32221439" rel="noopener noreferrer" target="_blank">How To Make It Rain (Part II)</a></p><p></p>
</div>

I also really liked what the guy in the podcast was saying. 
And I read another comment from you (Father) where you said that marketing can be just as creative as anything else...that making music is art, design and packaging is art, and telling the story of how something was created is art too. And I was like… wow, marketing can be art!! I’ve genuinely never seen it that way before.

I really loved this and I’m excited to read the other parts.</div>
